def: A finite set of ordered steps designed to solve a specific problem.
ex: The search {{algorithm}} ranks results by relevance and freshness.

def: The maximum rate of data transfer across a network connection over time.
ex: Streaming 4K video consumes a lot of {{bandwidth}} on the network.

def: A fast storage layer that keeps frequently accessed data for quick retrieval.
ex: Clearing the browser {{cache}} resolved the outdated page rendering.

def: A computer or program that provides resources or services to other machines.
ex: The web {{server}} returned a 500 error during peak traffic.

def: A device or program that requests services or data from a server.
ex: The mobile {{client}} sends a request to the API each minute.

def: A formal set of rules governing how data is exchanged between systems.
ex: HTTPS is the secure {{protocol}} used for encrypted web traffic.

def: A numerical label identifying a device on a network using the Internet Protocol.
ex: Each device on the LAN receives a unique {{IP address}} from the router.

def: A device that forwards data packets between different computer networks.
ex: The {{router}} directs traffic between the local network and the internet.

def: A security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic.
ex: The {{firewall}} blocked all inbound connections on unused ports.

def: The process of encoding data so only authorized parties can read it.
ex: End-to-end {{encryption}} protects messages from interception in transit.

def: An organized collection of structured data stored and accessed electronically.
ex: The user records are stored in a relational {{database}} on the server.

def: A request for data or information retrieved from a database.
ex: This SQL {{query}} returns all users who registered last month.

def: A copy of data kept to restore the original after loss or failure.
ex: A nightly {{backup}} ensures the database can be recovered after a crash.

def: On-demand computing resources delivered over the internet by remote servers.
ex: The company migrated its infrastructure to the {{cloud}} last quarter.

def: Creating virtual versions of hardware, storage, or networks from physical resources.
ex: {{Virtualization}} lets one physical server host several isolated machines.

def: The time delay between a request and the corresponding response in a system.
ex: High {{latency}} made the online game feel sluggish and unresponsive.

def: The amount of data or work successfully processed within a given time.
ex: Adding worker nodes doubled the system's {{throughput}} under heavy load.

def: A formatted unit of data transmitted across a packet-switched network.
ex: The analyzer captured every {{packet}} sent over the network interface.

def: A system that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.
ex: A misconfigured {{DNS}} record prevented users from reaching the website.

def: A network node that connects two networks using different protocols.
ex: All outbound traffic passes through the default {{gateway}} first.

def: The physical components that make up a computer or electronic system.
ex: The server crashed because of a faulty {{hardware}} component.

def: Programs and instructions that tell a computer how to operate.
ex: The new {{software}} release fixed several long-standing security issues.

def: Core software that manages hardware resources and runs application programs.
ex: The application runs on any modern {{operating system}} without changes.

def: The central component of an operating system managing hardware and processes.
ex: A driver bug caused the {{kernel}} to panic and reboot.

def: Software that lets the operating system communicate with a hardware device.
ex: Installing the latest {{driver}} fixed the graphics card compatibility problem.

def: A shared boundary where two systems or a user and system interact.
ex: The redesigned user {{interface}} made the app far easier to navigate.

def: A set of rules letting software components communicate and exchange data.
ex: The mobile app fetches user data through a REST {{API}}.

def: A flaw in software that causes incorrect or unexpected behavior.
ex: A critical {{bug}} caused the checkout page to crash on submit.

def: A small software update that fixes bugs or security vulnerabilities.
ex: The vendor released a security {{patch}} to close the vulnerability.

def: A newer version of software adding features or fixing existing issues.
ex: The latest {{update}} improved battery life and stability.

def: Low-level software permanently programmed into a hardware device.
ex: Updating the router's {{firmware}} patched a critical security flaw.

def: Software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized system access.
ex: The antivirus quarantined the {{malware}} before it could spread.

def: The process of verifying the identity of a user or system.
ex: Two-factor {{authentication}} adds an extra layer of account security.

def: Granting a verified user permission to access specific resources or actions.
ex: The API returned a 403 error due to insufficient {{authorization}}.

def: A secret string of characters used to verify a user's identity.
ex: Users must choose a strong {{password}} with at least twelve characters.

def: A numbered endpoint used to direct network traffic to specific services.
ex: The web server listens for connections on {{port}} 443.

def: A network connecting devices within a limited area like an office.
ex: All office printers are shared across the {{LAN}}.

def: A network spanning a large geographic area, often connecting multiple sites.
ex: The company links its regional offices over a private {{WAN}}.

def: An encrypted connection that securely links a device to a private network.
ex: Remote employees connect through a {{VPN}} to access internal systems.

def: An intermediary server that forwards requests between clients and other servers.
ex: The {{proxy}} caches responses to reduce load on the origin server.

def: The smallest unit of digital data, holding a value of zero or one.
ex: A single {{bit}} can represent either a zero or a one.

def: A unit of digital information consisting of eight bits.
ex: Each character in ASCII is stored using one {{byte}}.

def: A base-two number system using only the digits zero and one.
ex: Computers store all data internally as {{binary}}.

def: Reducing the size of data to save storage or transmission bandwidth.
ex: Image {{compression}} shrank the file size by seventy percent.

def: The period during which a system remains operational and available.
ex: The service guarantees 99.9 percent {{uptime}} in its agreement.

def: A period when a system is unavailable or not functioning.
ex: Scheduled maintenance caused two hours of planned {{downtime}}.

def: Distributing incoming traffic across multiple servers to optimize performance.
ex: {{Load balancing}} spreads requests evenly across the server cluster.

def: A software emulation of a physical computer running its own operating system.
ex: Developers test the app inside an isolated {{virtual machine}}.

def: A lightweight, isolated package bundling an application with its dependencies.
ex: The service runs in a Docker {{container}} for consistent deployment.

def: The process of releasing software into a live production environment.
ex: The automated {{deployment}} pushed the new build to production.

def: The arrangement of settings that determine how a system operates.
ex: A wrong {{configuration}} value broke the database connection.

def: A text interface used to enter commands and interact with a system.
ex: Open the {{terminal}} and run the installation command.

def: A text-based interface where users type commands to control a computer.
ex: You can deploy the app directly from the {{command line}}.

def: A short program that automates a sequence of tasks or commands.
ex: A Python {{script}} automates the nightly database backup.

def: A group of interconnected computers that share resources and data.
ex: The office {{network}} connects hundreds of devices securely.

def: A single connection point or device within a network or system.
ex: Each {{node}} in the cluster processes part of the workload.

def: A computer connected to a network that provides or accesses resources.
ex: The application connects to a remote {{host}} over SSH.

def: A human-readable name identifying a website or network resource.
ex: The company registered a new {{domain}} for its product launch.

def: A small data file a website stores in the user's browser.
ex: The site uses a {{cookie}} to remember your login session.

def: A temporary interactive exchange between a user and a system.
ex: The user's {{session}} expired after fifteen minutes of inactivity.

def: A test measuring the round-trip time for messages to a host.
ex: A high {{ping}} caused noticeable lag during the video call.

def: The capacity to retain digital data for later retrieval and use.
ex: The server ran out of {{storage}} during the large backup.

def: Volatile memory that temporarily stores data for active programs.
ex: The application crashed after consuming all available {{RAM}}.

def: The main processor that executes instructions and performs computations.
ex: Rendering the video pushed {{CPU}} usage to one hundred percent.

def: Intentionally limiting network speed to control or reduce data transfer.
ex: The provider applies {{bandwidth throttling}} once you exceed the data cap.

def: A facility housing servers and infrastructure for storing and processing data.
ex: The company's {{data center}} handles millions of requests each day.

def: Working memory that temporarily holds data for currently running processes.
ex: Insufficient {{operating memory}} slowed the system to a crawl.

def: A logical subdivision of an IP network into smaller, manageable segments.
ex: The admin split the office network into a separate {{subnet}} for printers.

def: A 32-bit value that separates the network and host portions of an IP address.
ex: Set the {{subnet mask}} to 255.255.255.0 for a standard class C network.

def: The router node that forwards traffic from a local network to other networks.
ex: If the {{default gateway}} is misconfigured, devices cannot reach the internet.

def: A protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses to devices on a network.
ex: The router runs a {{DHCP}} server to lease addresses to new clients.

def: A connection-oriented protocol ensuring reliable, ordered delivery of data packets.
ex: Web traffic relies on {{TCP}} to guarantee that no packets are lost.

def: A connectionless protocol offering fast, unreliable delivery without acknowledgements.
ex: Video streaming often uses {{UDP}} for lower latency despite occasional loss.

def: The application protocol used for transferring web pages between servers and browsers.
ex: The browser sent an {{HTTP}} request to load the homepage.

def: A secure version of HTTP that encrypts data using TLS or SSL.
ex: Online banking sites always use {{HTTPS}} to protect user credentials.

def: A standard protocol for transferring files between a client and a server.
ex: Developers used {{FTP}} to upload the website files to the hosting server.

def: A protocol for securely accessing and managing remote machines over a network.
ex: The engineer connected to the server via {{SSH}} to restart the service.

def: An older cryptographic protocol for securing communications, now largely replaced by TLS.
ex: The legacy application still relied on an outdated {{SSL}} certificate.

def: A modern cryptographic protocol that encrypts data transmitted over networks.
ex: The server negotiates a {{TLS}} handshake before any data is exchanged.

def: A unique hardware identifier assigned to a network interface for communications.
ex: The firewall filters devices by their {{MAC address}} to block intruders.

def: A widely used wired networking technology for connecting devices in local networks.
ex: The desktop is connected through {{ethernet}} for a stable, fast connection.

def: A wireless technology allowing devices to connect to a network via radio signals.
ex: Guests can join the office {{Wi-Fi}} using the password on the poster.

def: A physical location or device offering wireless internet access to nearby users.
ex: She turned her phone into a {{hotspot}} to get her laptop online.

def: A device that modulates and demodulates signals to connect a network to the internet.
ex: The technician replaced the old {{modem}} to improve the broadband speed.

def: A device that connects devices within a network and forwards data by MAC address.
ex: The office added a 48-port {{switch}} to connect all the new workstations.

def: A basic device that broadcasts incoming data to all connected network ports.
ex: An old {{hub}} floods every port with traffic, unlike a modern switch.

def: A device that lets wireless clients connect to a wired network.
ex: They mounted an {{access point}} on the ceiling to extend Wi-Fi coverage.

def: The physical or logical arrangement of nodes and connections in a network.
ex: They chose a star {{network topology}} to simplify troubleshooting.

def: The failure of data packets to reach their destination across a network.
ex: High {{packet loss}} caused the video call to freeze repeatedly.

def: The variation in delay between packets arriving over a network connection.
ex: Excessive {{jitter}} made the audio in the conference sound choppy.

def: A diagnostic tool that maps the path packets take to a destination.
ex: Run a {{traceroute}} to find which hop is dropping the connection.

def: Redirecting network traffic from one port or address to another internal host.
ex: Enable {{port forwarding}} so the game server is reachable from outside.

def: A technique that maps private IP addresses to a public one for internet access.
ex: The router uses {{NAT}} to let many devices share a single public IP.

def: The fourth-version internet protocol using 32-bit numerical addresses.
ex: Most home networks still assign {{IPv4}} addresses like 192.168.1.1.

def: The newer internet protocol using 128-bit addresses to expand address space.
ex: Switching to {{IPv6}} solves the shortage of available IPv4 addresses.

def: A server that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.
ex: The site failed to load because the {{DNS server}} was unreachable.

def: A human-readable address identifying a website or resource on the internet.
ex: They registered the {{domain name}} example.com for the new startup.

def: A complete web address specifying the location of a resource online.
ex: Paste the {{URL}} into the browser bar to open the report.

def: A server that stores and delivers web pages to clients over HTTP.
ex: The {{web server}} crashed under the sudden spike in visitors.

def: A server that sends, receives, and stores email messages for users.
ex: The {{mail server}} rejected the message because the mailbox was full.

def: An intermediary server that forwards requests between clients and other servers.
ex: Employees browse the web through a {{proxy server}} for security and caching.

def: A device or service that distributes traffic across multiple servers evenly.
ex: The {{load balancer}} routes users to the least busy application server.

def: A standardized frame for mounting and organizing servers and networking equipment.
ex: The data center installed a new {{rack}} to house the extra servers.

def: The main circuit board connecting and coordinating a computer's core components.
ex: A faulty {{motherboard}} can prevent the whole computer from powering on.

def: The chip that executes instructions and performs the computer's main calculations.
ex: The new {{processor}} handles video rendering far faster than the old one.

def: A hardware component that renders images and video for display output.
ex: Gamers upgraded the {{graphics card}} to run titles at higher resolution.

def: A unit that converts mains electricity into voltages the computer components need.
ex: An underpowered {{power supply}} may cause the system to shut down randomly.

def: A storage device using spinning magnetic disks to hold data persistently.
ex: The old laptop's {{hard drive}} was slow and nearly out of space.

def: A fast storage device using flash memory with no moving parts.
ex: Installing a {{solid-state drive}} made the computer boot in seconds.

def: Non-volatile storage that retains data without power and erases electronically.
ex: USB sticks use {{flash memory}} to store files without any moving parts.

def: An external device connected to a computer to add functionality or input.
ex: The keyboard is a {{peripheral}} connected to the laptop via USB.

def: A standard connector for linking peripherals and transferring data or power.
ex: Plug the drive into any free {{USB}} port to copy the files.

def: A digital interface for transmitting high-definition video and audio signals.
ex: Connect the laptop to the monitor using an {{HDMI}} cable.

def: A physical cable used to connect devices for wired network communication.
ex: Run an {{ethernet cable}} to the desk for a more reliable connection.

def: Relating to cables that transmit data as light through thin glass strands.
ex: The city upgraded to {{fiber optic}} lines for gigabit internet speeds.

def: A network that connects devices using radio waves instead of cables.
ex: The cafe offers a free {{wireless network}} for all its customers.

def: A hardware component that lets a computer connect to a network.
ex: The server has a dual-port {{network interface card}} for redundancy.

def: To start up a computer and load its operating system into memory.
ex: The machine takes about ten seconds to {{boot}} into Windows.

def: To restart a computer so it shuts down and boots again.
ex: Please {{reboot}} the router if the connection keeps dropping.

def: Firmware that initializes hardware and starts the operating system during boot.
ex: Enter the {{BIOS}} settings to change the boot device order.

def: Small, fast memory storing frequently accessed data to speed up processing.
ex: The processor keeps recent instructions in {{cache memory}} for quick access.

def: A feature allowing a user to control another computer over a network.
ex: The IT team used {{remote desktop}} to fix the issue from the office.

def: A service that stores data on remote servers accessible over the internet.
ex: The team backs up all documents to {{cloud storage}} every night.

def: Network techniques that prioritize traffic to guarantee performance for critical applications.
ex: Enable {{quality of service}} to give video calls priority over downloads.

def: Dividing a network into smaller parts to improve security and performance.
ex: {{Network segmentation}} keeps the guest Wi-Fi isolated from internal servers.

def: High-speed internet access that transmits large amounts of data quickly.
ex: The village finally got {{broadband}} after years of slow dial-up.

def: A sudden, temporary increase in the delay of network responses.
ex: A {{latency spike}} during peak hours made the game briefly unplayable.

def: A unit of digital storage equal to about one thousand bytes.
ex: A short plain-text note might be only a few {{kilobyte}} in size.

def: A unit of digital storage equal to about one million bytes.
ex: A high-quality photo can take several {{megabyte}} of storage.

def: A unit of digital storage equal to about one billion bytes.
ex: The movie download was nearly two {{gigabyte}} in total.

def: A unit of digital storage equal to about one trillion bytes.
ex: The new drive offers four {{terabyte}} of backup capacity.

def: The set of chips managing data flow between the processor and other components.
ex: The {{motherboard chipset}} determines which processors the board supports.

def: A metal component that draws heat away from electronic parts to cool them.
ex: The {{heat sink}} keeps the processor from overheating under heavy load.

def: A fan that circulates air to lower the temperature of computer components.
ex: The {{cooling fan}} spins faster when the laptop gets hot.

def: A visual interface using icons, windows and menus instead of text commands.
ex: The new app offers an intuitive {{graphical user interface}} that beginners can navigate easily.

def: A text-based interface where users type commands to operate a computer.
ex: Experienced developers often prefer the {{command-line interface}} for faster server management.

def: The method an operating system uses to store and organize files.
ex: The corrupted {{file system}} prevented the server from reading any stored data.

def: A named folder that organizes and contains files on a computer.
ex: Move the configuration file into the root {{directory}} before restarting the service.

def: A file containing a program that a computer can run directly.
ex: Double-click the {{executable}} to launch the installation wizard on Windows.

def: The process of copying and configuring software so it can run.
ex: The {{installation}} failed because the disk did not have enough free space.

def: To remove installed software completely from a computer or device.
ex: Please {{uninstall}} the old version before installing the latest release.

def: New software that improves how hardware communicates with the operating system.
ex: A recent {{driver update}} fixed the flickering issue on the external monitor.

def: New low-level software installed onto a device's permanent memory.
ex: The router regained stability after we applied the latest {{firmware update}}.

def: The minimum hardware and software needed to run a program properly.
ex: Check the {{system requirements}} before purchasing the game for your laptop.

def: The ability of software or hardware to work correctly together.
ex: We tested {{compatibility}} between the plugin and several browser versions.

def: A small software component that adds features to an existing program.
ex: Install the payment {{plugin}} to accept credit cards on your website.

def: Add-on software that extends the functionality of a browser or application.
ex: This browser {{extension}} blocks ads and speeds up page loading.

def: An optional component installed to add features to existing software.
ex: The premium {{add-on}} unlocks advanced reporting tools in the dashboard.

def: Software used to access, view and navigate pages on the internet.
ex: Clear the cache in your {{web browser}} if the page fails to load.

def: An online tool that finds web pages matching a user's query.
ex: Optimizing content helps a site rank higher in any {{search engine}}.

def: A clickable element that takes users to another page or resource.
ex: Add a {{hyperlink}} to the documentation so readers can learn more.

def: A saved link that lets users quickly return to a web page.
ex: Save this dashboard as a {{bookmark}} for faster access each morning.

def: To copy data from a remote server onto a local device.
ex: Users can {{download}} the installer directly from the official website.

def: To send data from a local device to a remote server.
ex: Please {{upload}} the report to the shared drive before the meeting.

def: Delivering audio or video continuously so it plays while downloading.
ex: Live {{streaming}} requires stable bandwidth to avoid quality drops.

def: Preloading media data into memory to ensure smooth playback.
ex: Constant {{buffering}} suggests the connection is too slow for high-definition video.

def: The number of pixels that define the detail of a display image.
ex: Increase the screen {{resolution}} for sharper text and clearer graphics.

def: The smallest single point of color in a digital image.
ex: A dead {{pixel}} appeared as a black dot on the display.

def: The process of generating a final image or page from data.
ex: Slow {{rendering}} of the chart delayed the dashboard's initial load.

def: The part of an application that users see and interact with.
ex: Our {{front end}} was rebuilt in React to improve responsiveness.

def: The server-side part of an application handling logic and data.
ex: The {{back end}} processes payments and stores customer records securely.

def: Covering both front-end and back-end development of an application.
ex: A {{full stack}} developer can build both the interface and the server.

def: A service that stores websites and makes them accessible online.
ex: We migrated to faster {{web hosting}} to reduce page load times.

def: Software that lets users create and manage digital content without coding.
ex: The marketing team updates the blog through a {{content management system}}.

def: Software that stores, retrieves and manages data in databases.
ex: The {{database management system}} handles thousands of concurrent queries per second.

def: Improving database queries so they run faster and use fewer resources.
ex: Careful {{query optimization}} cut the report generation time in half.

def: Creating data structures that speed up database search and retrieval.
ex: Adding {{indexing}} on the email column dramatically improved lookup speed.

def: Moving data from one system, format or storage to another.
ex: The {{data migration}} to the new cloud platform took an entire weekend.

def: Copying data and restoring it after loss, failure or corruption.
ex: A solid {{backup and recovery}} strategy protects the company from data loss.

def: Plans and processes to restore systems after a major failure.
ex: The {{disaster recovery}} plan restored operations within two hours of the outage.

def: Duplicating critical components so a system keeps running after failure.
ex: Built-in {{redundancy}} ensures the service stays online even if a server fails.

def: Automatically switching to a backup system when the primary fails.
ex: Automatic {{failover}} redirected traffic to the secondary data center instantly.

def: Software that creates and runs multiple virtual machines on one host.
ex: The {{hypervisor}} allocates memory and CPU across several virtual servers.

def: Delivering computing services over the internet instead of local servers.
ex: Adopting {{cloud computing}} let the startup scale without buying hardware.

def: A cloud model providing virtual servers, storage and networking on demand.
ex: Using {{infrastructure as a service}}, we rent servers instead of owning them.

def: A cloud model offering tools to build and deploy applications.
ex: With {{platform as a service}}, developers deploy code without managing servers.

def: Software delivered online by subscription rather than installed locally.
ex: The company switched to {{software as a service}} to avoid maintaining local apps.

def: Cloud resources shared and delivered by a third-party provider online.
ex: Hosting the website on a {{public cloud}} reduced upfront infrastructure costs.

def: Cloud infrastructure dedicated to and controlled by a single organization.
ex: The bank chose a {{private cloud}} to keep sensitive data fully isolated.

def: A mix of public and private cloud working as one environment.
ex: Their {{hybrid cloud}} keeps sensitive data private while scaling publicly for peaks.

def: A model where the provider manages servers and runs code on demand.
ex: We built the API using {{serverless}} functions that scale automatically with traffic.

def: Processing data near its source to reduce latency and bandwidth.
ex: {{Edge computing}} lets sensors analyze data locally before sending summaries to the cloud.

def: Packaging an application with its dependencies into a portable container.
ex: {{Containerization}} with Docker made the app run identically across every environment.

def: Automated coordination and management of multiple containers or services.
ex: Kubernetes handles the {{orchestration}} of hundreds of containers across the cluster.

def: Using technology to perform tasks with minimal human intervention.
ex: Test {{automation}} lets the team catch bugs before every release.

def: A support team that helps users resolve technical problems.
ex: Contact the {{help desk}} if you cannot reset your password.

def: Software that logs, tracks and manages user support requests.
ex: Every issue is logged in the {{ticketing system}} and assigned to an agent.

def: Systematically diagnosing and solving problems in hardware or software.
ex: Basic {{troubleshooting}} often begins with restarting the affected device.

def: Investigating an incident to find its underlying, fundamental cause.
ex: The {{root cause analysis}} revealed a misconfigured firewall caused the outage.

def: A professional who manages, configures and maintains computer systems.
ex: The {{system administrator}} granted new employees access to the shared servers.

def: Permissions that define what resources a user can view or modify.
ex: Review each employee's {{access rights}} to prevent unauthorized data exposure.

def: An identity that lets a person log in and use a system.
ex: Create a {{user account}} for each new team member before onboarding.

def: One login that grants access to multiple related applications.
ex: With {{single sign-on}}, employees use one password for all company tools.

def: Security requiring two separate proofs of identity to log in.
ex: Enabling {{two-factor authentication}} adds a code sent to your phone.

def: An electronic credential that verifies identity and enables secure connections.
ex: The website presents a valid {{digital certificate}} to confirm it is authentic.

def: A code that unlocks and authorizes the use of licensed software.
ex: Enter your {{license key}} to activate the full version of the program.

def: The person who ultimately uses a product or piece of software.
ex: We collected feedback from every {{end user}} before releasing the update.

def: The ability to connect to and use a system from elsewhere.
ex: {{Remote access}} lets engineers fix the server without visiting the office.

def: Keeping data consistent and identical across multiple devices or systems.
ex: {{Data synchronization}} ensures your notes appear the same on phone and laptop.

def: New software that fixes bugs and improves an operating system.
ex: Install the latest {{system update}} to patch the reported security flaw.

def: A notification informing the user that something went wrong.
ex: The {{error message}} said the file could not be found on the server.

def: A sudden failure that causes a program or system to stop.
ex: The app suffered a {{crash}} whenever users uploaded very large files.

def: A state where a program stops responding to any user input.
ex: A memory leak caused the screen {{freeze}} during the presentation.

def: A file that records events and activities within a system.
ex: Check the {{log file}} to see exactly when the service crashed.

def: An instance of a running program with its own memory and resources.
ex: Each running application starts at least one {{process}} in memory.

def: The smallest unit of execution that runs within a process.
ex: A single process can spawn many a {{thread}} for parallel work.

def: A background process that runs without direct user interaction.
ex: The logging {{daemon}} quietly records events around the clock.

def: A hierarchical database storing configuration settings for the operating system.
ex: Editing the Windows {{registry}} can break the system if careless.

def: A named value that affects how running processes behave on a system.
ex: Set the PATH {{environment variable}} so the shell finds executables.

def: A program that interprets user commands and passes them to the system.
ex: Bash is the default {{shell}} on most Linux distributions.

def: The privileged account with full control over a Unix-like system.
ex: Only the {{root user}} may modify protected system files directly.

def: A command scheduled to run automatically at fixed times on Unix.
ex: A nightly {{cron job}} backs up the database at two o'clock.

def: A component that decides which process or task runs next.
ex: The kernel {{scheduler}} allocates CPU time fairly across processes.

def: Disk area used as overflow when physical memory is exhausted.
ex: Adding more {{swap space}} prevents crashes under heavy memory load.

def: A file on disk that Windows uses to extend physical memory.
ex: Windows moves idle memory pages into the {{page file}} on disk.

def: A directory where a filesystem or device is attached and accessed.
ex: The USB drive appears under the /media {{mount point}} automatically.

def: A logically separate section of a physical storage drive.
ex: He created a separate {{partition}} for the operating system files.

def: A special file that points to another file or directory path.
ex: Create a {{symbolic link}} so both paths reach the same file.

def: A tool showing running processes and letting users end them.
ex: Open the {{task manager}} to see which process consumes the CPU.

def: A background program that starts automatically and runs continuously.
ex: Restart the print {{service}} to fix the stalled queue.

def: Automatic archiving and clearing of log files to save disk space.
ex: Configure {{log rotation}} so old logs are compressed weekly.

def: Centralized rules controlling users and computers in a Windows domain.
ex: A {{group policy}} enforces password complexity across every workstation.

def: A request from a program to the kernel for a service.
ex: Reading a file triggers a {{system call}} into the kernel.

def: Rules defining who may read, write, or execute a file.
ex: Change the {{file permission}} so only the owner can edit it.

def: A unique number the system assigns to each running process.
ex: Use the {{process ID}} to send a signal to the program.

def: A process that has created one or more other processes.
ex: When the {{parent process}} exits, its children may be reassigned.

def: A process created by another process called its parent.
ex: The shell forks a {{child process}} to run each command.

def: Running several processes seemingly at once by sharing CPU time.
ex: Modern operating systems support {{multitasking}} across dozens of apps.

def: Executing multiple threads within a single process concurrently.
ex: {{Multithreading}} lets the browser render while downloading files.

def: Saving one process's state and loading another's on the CPU.
ex: Frequent {{context switch}} operations add measurable overhead to performance.

def: A state where processes wait forever for each other's resources.
ex: Two threads locking resources in reverse order caused a {{deadlock}}.

def: A counter controlling access to a shared resource among processes.
ex: A {{semaphore}} limits how many threads enter the section.

def: A lock ensuring only one thread accesses a resource at once.
ex: Guard the shared counter with a {{mutex}} to avoid corruption.

def: A bug where output depends on unpredictable timing of threads.
ex: A {{race condition}} corrupted the counter under heavy load.

def: A technique giving each process its own large address space.
ex: {{Virtual memory}} lets programs use more space than physical RAM.

def: Managing memory in fixed blocks moved between RAM and disk.
ex: Excessive {{paging}} slows the system to a crawl.

def: Memory that a program allocates but never releases back.
ex: A slow {{memory leak}} eventually exhausted all available RAM.

def: Automatic reclaiming of memory no longer used by a program.
ex: {{Garbage collection}} frees objects the code no longer references.

def: A small program that loads the operating system at startup.
ex: The {{boot loader}} lets you pick which system to start.

def: The first process that starts and manages all system services.
ex: Systemd is the {{init system}} on many Linux distributions.

def: A mode defining which services run on a Unix system.
ex: Switching to single-user {{runlevel}} stops most background services.

def: An account with unrestricted administrative access to the system.
ex: Only a {{superuser}} can install packages system-wide.

def: A command that runs tasks with elevated administrative privileges temporarily.
ex: Prefix the command with {{sudo}} to gain root rights.

def: A collection of accounts sharing common permissions and access rights.
ex: Add the account to the sudo {{user group}} for access.

def: A personal folder where a user's files and settings live.
ex: Configuration files are stored in each user's {{home directory}}.

def: The folder a process currently treats as its base location.
ex: Relative paths resolve against the current {{working directory}}.

def: The default stream where a program writes normal results.
ex: Redirect {{standard output}} to a file with the arrow.

def: The default stream from which a program reads incoming data.
ex: The command reads text from {{standard input}} until end-of-file.

def: A mechanism sending one command's output to another's input.
ex: Use a {{pipe}} to feed the list into grep.

def: Sending a stream to a file or device instead of default.
ex: Output {{redirection}} saves the command's results to a log.

def: A symbol standing for one or more unspecified characters.
ex: The {{wildcard}} asterisk matches every file in the folder.

def: A directory entry pointing directly to a file's inode.
ex: A {{hard link}} shares the same data as the original file.

def: An integer the kernel uses to reference an open file.
ex: The process ran out of available {{file descriptor}} slots.

def: A data structure storing metadata about a filesystem object.
ex: Each file's {{inode}} records its permissions and disk location.

def: A filesystem feature logging changes to survive sudden crashes.
ex: {{Journaling}} lets the filesystem recover quickly after a power failure.

def: A limit on storage space a user or group may consume.
ex: An exceeded {{disk quota}} blocks the user from saving files.

def: Combining several disks for redundancy or improved performance.
ex: A {{RAID}} array keeps data safe if one disk fails.

def: A flexible storage unit spanning one or more physical disks.
ex: Resize the {{logical volume}} without repartitioning the underlying disks.

def: Reorganizing scattered file fragments to speed up disk access.
ex: {{Defragmentation}} rearranges files so the drive reads them faster.

def: A saved state of a system or disk at one moment.
ex: Take a {{snapshot}} before upgrading so you can revert.

def: A process that runs without occupying the interactive terminal.
ex: Append an ampersand to run the job as a {{background process}}.

def: A finished process whose exit status the parent hasn't collected.
ex: A {{zombie process}} lingers until its parent reads the status.

def: A running process whose parent has already terminated.
ex: An {{orphan process}} is adopted by the init system.

def: A notification sent to a process to trigger predefined behavior.
ex: Send a terminate {{signal}} to stop the running process.

def: A hardware or software event that halts the CPU temporarily.
ex: A keyboard {{interrupt}} tells the CPU a key was pressed.

def: A number setting the scheduling priority of a Unix process.
ex: Lower the {{nice value}} to give the process more CPU.

def: A measure of how many processes wait for CPU time.
ex: A high {{load average}} suggests the server is overworked.

def: A record of significant system and application events over time.
ex: Check the {{event log}} to diagnose the unexpected shutdown.

def: A standard protocol and service for collecting system log messages.
ex: Forward every server's {{syslog}} to a central collector.

def: A tool that installs, updates, and removes software packages.
ex: Use the {{package manager}} to install the missing library.

def: A component another program requires in order to function.
ex: Installing the app pulled in every missing {{dependency}} automatically.

def: Reverting a system to a previous known-good state.
ex: The failed update triggered an automatic {{rollback}} to yesterday.

def: The orderly process of powering off a computer safely.
ex: Save your work before the scheduled {{shutdown}} at midnight.

def: Save memory to disk and power down for later resume.
ex: Laptops {{hibernate}} to preserve the session while using no power.

def: An action set to run automatically at a defined time.
ex: A {{scheduled task}} cleans temporary files every Sunday night.

def: A script of commands executed sequentially by the Windows shell.
ex: Run the {{batch file}} to automate the whole setup.

def: A short custom name standing in for a longer command.
ex: Define an {{alias}} so a short word runs the command.

def: A table specifying which users may access particular resources.
ex: The {{access control list}} grants read rights to the team.

def: Gaining higher access rights than normally granted to a user.
ex: The exploit allowed {{privilege escalation}} to full administrator rights.

def: A server that authenticates users within a network domain.
ex: The {{domain controller}} validates every login across the office.

def: Microsoft's directory service managing users, computers, and permissions.
ex: {{Active directory}} stores every account and group in the domain.

def: Confining a process to a restricted apparent root directory.
ex: Running the service in a {{chroot}} limits filesystem access.

def: A kernel feature isolating resources so processes see separate views.
ex: Each container runs inside its own network {{namespace}}.

def: A timer that restarts the system if software stops responding.
ex: The {{watchdog}} reboots the device when the process hangs.

def: A process that occupies the terminal and receives user input.
ex: The {{foreground process}} blocks the shell until it finishes.

def: Forcibly terminate a running program that misbehaves or hangs.
ex: You can {{kill process}} instances that no longer respond.

def: Attaching a filesystem to the directory tree for access.
ex: {{Mounting}} the drive makes its files appear under a folder.

def: The amount of storage space occupied on a drive.
ex: Check {{disk usage}} before installing the large application.

def: The top-level directory containing all others on a filesystem.
ex: Every path on Unix begins at the {{root directory}}.

def: The process of applying software updates across many systems.
ex: Good {{patch management}} keeps every server free of vulnerabilities.

def: A stored set of settings and data specific to one account.
ex: Corrupted {{user profile}} data prevented the account from loading.

def: The shell started when a user first logs into a system.
ex: His account uses Zsh as the default {{login shell}}.

def: A text interface for entering commands and viewing system output.
ex: Log in at the {{console}} when the network is down.

def: Describing a system running without a monitor or display.
ex: The {{headless}} server is managed entirely over the network.

def: Designing systems to stay operational with minimal unplanned downtime.
ex: A {{high availability}} cluster survives the loss of one node.

def: A structured set of data organised into rows and columns in a database.
ex: Customer details are stored in a single {{table}} with several columns.

def: A single row in a table representing one complete set of related data.
ex: Each {{record}} in the table holds one customer's information.

def: A single column or attribute that stores one piece of data in a record.
ex: The email {{field}} must contain a valid address for every record.

def: A field uniquely identifying each record within a database table.
ex: The user ID serves as the {{primary key}} for this table.

def: A field linking one table to the primary key of another table.
ex: The order table uses a {{foreign key}} to reference each customer.

def: A structure that speeds up data retrieval from a database table.
ex: Adding an {{index}} on the name column made searches much faster.

def: A precompiled set of SQL statements stored and executed inside the database.
ex: We moved the logic into a {{stored procedure}} to improve performance.

def: A group of database operations executed as a single, all-or-nothing unit.
ex: The bank transfer runs inside one {{transaction}} to ensure consistency.

def: Permanently saving all changes made during a database transaction.
ex: Once you {{commit}}, the changes become visible to other users.

def: A dedicated storage device serving files to clients over a network.
ex: The team shares project files through a {{network-attached storage}} device.

def: A high-speed network giving servers block-level access to shared storage.
ex: The data centre relies on a {{storage area network}} for its databases.

def: A collection of hard drives managed together as one storage system.
ex: The {{disk array}} provides several terabytes of reliable capacity.

def: A logical storage unit that the operating system treats as a drive.
ex: We mounted a new {{volume}} to store the database files separately.

def: A store of older data kept long-term but rarely accessed.
ex: Old invoices are moved to an {{archive}} after two years.

def: A central repository storing integrated data for analysis and reporting.
ex: Sales figures flow into a {{data warehouse}} for business reporting.

def: The delay before changes on a primary database reach its replicas.
ex: High {{replication lag}} meant the replica showed stale data.

def: A cache of reusable database connections shared among application requests.
ex: The {{connection pool}} reduces the cost of opening new connections.

def: Software mapping database tables to objects in application code.
ex: The {{ORM}} lets developers query the database without writing raw SQL.

def: A versioned change applied to a database schema over time.
ex: Each {{migration}} adds or alters tables in a controlled way.

def: Planning the structure of tables, fields, and relationships in a database.
ex: Good {{schema design}} avoids duplicated data and slow queries.

def: The strategy the database engine uses to execute a query.
ex: Reading the {{query plan}} revealed a missing index on the join.

def: When requested data is found already stored in the cache.
ex: A high {{cache hit}} rate keeps response times very low.

def: When requested data is not in the cache and must be fetched.
ex: Each {{cache miss}} forces a slower read from the main database.

def: The formal structure defining tables, fields, and relationships in a database.
ex: The {{schema}} describes how every table relates to the others.

def: Organising data to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.
ex: Proper {{normalization}} split the data into several related tables.

def: Adding redundant data to a database to speed up read queries.
ex: We used {{denormalization}} to avoid expensive joins on the report.

def: An operation combining rows from two or more related tables.
ex: A {{join}} merges customer and order data into one result set.

def: A virtual table defined by a saved query over real tables.
ex: The {{view}} presents active customers without exposing the full table.

def: Code that runs automatically when a specified database event occurs.
ex: A {{trigger}} updates the log whenever a record is deleted.

def: A rule limiting the values allowed in a database column.
ex: A unique {{constraint}} prevents two users sharing the same email.

def: The accuracy and consistency of data throughout its lifecycle.
ex: Constraints protect {{data integrity}} by rejecting invalid entries.

def: A database category storing data without a fixed relational schema.
ex: They chose a {{NoSQL}} store for its flexible document structure.

def: A database storing data in related tables with defined relationships.
ex: A {{relational database}} keeps orders and customers in linked tables.

def: A database storing data as flexible, self-describing documents like JSON.
ex: The {{document store}} keeps each profile as one JSON document.

def: A simple database mapping unique keys to their associated values.
ex: A {{key-value store}} caches sessions using the user ID as the key.

def: Splitting a large database across multiple servers by partitioning data.
ex: {{Sharding}} spreads the users across four database servers.

def: Grouping servers to work together for scalability and availability.
ex: Database {{clustering}} keeps the service running if one node fails.

def: A copy of a database kept in sync with the primary.
ex: Read queries are sent to a {{replica}} to reduce the primary's load.

def: A large store holding raw data in its native format.
ex: Sensor readings pour into a {{data lake}} before any processing.

def: Storage managing data as objects with metadata and unique identifiers.
ex: Images are kept in cloud {{object storage}} rather than on disk.

def: Storage dividing data into fixed-size blocks addressed individually.
ex: The database sits on fast {{block storage}} attached to the server.

def: Storage organising data as files within a hierarchy of folders.
ex: Shared documents live in network {{file storage}} for the whole team.

def: An abstract structure defining how data elements relate to each other.
ex: The {{data model}} shows how orders link to products and customers.

def: A distinct object or concept represented as a table in a database.
ex: Each {{entity}}, such as customer or product, becomes its own table.

def: A named property of an entity, stored as a column value.
ex: The price {{attribute}} describes each product record.

def: A storage location where the database physically keeps its tables.
ex: Large tables were moved to a separate {{tablespace}} on faster disks.

def: A classification specifying what kind of value a field can hold.
ex: Choose the integer {{data type}} for the quantity column.

def: A marker indicating that a field has no data recorded.
ex: A {{null value}} means the phone number is simply unknown.

def: A function computing a single result from a set of values.
ex: An {{aggregate function}} like SUM totals every order amount.

def: A mechanism preventing concurrent access to a data item.
ex: The row {{lock}} stops two users editing the same record at once.

def: Multiple transactions accessing the database at the same time.
ex: High {{concurrency}} requires careful locking to avoid conflicts.

def: Ensuring foreign keys always point to existing related records.
ex: {{Referential integrity}} blocks deleting a customer with open orders.

def: A repository describing the structure and meaning of database objects.
ex: The {{data dictionary}} lists every table, column, and data type.

def: Data describing other data, such as its structure or origin.
ex: File {{metadata}} records the author, size, and creation date.

def: The scheduled time period during which backups are performed.
ex: The nightly {{backup window}} runs while system usage is lowest.

def: The policy defining how long data is kept before deletion.
ex: Our {{data retention}} policy keeps logs for exactly ninety days.

def: Low-cost storage for rarely accessed data with slower retrieval.
ex: Archived records sit in {{cold storage}} until legally required.

def: Fast storage for frequently accessed data needing quick retrieval.
ex: Active user files stay in {{hot storage}} for instant access.

def: Estimating future storage needs to ensure sufficient resources.
ex: {{Capacity planning}} predicted we would need more disks by autumn.

def: Eliminating duplicate copies of data to save storage space.
ex: {{Deduplication}} cut the backup size almost in half.

def: Allocating storage or compute resources for a system to use.
ex: Automatic {{provisioning}} adds disk space when the volume fills up.

def: A named collection of database files managed as a storage unit.
ex: Indexes were placed in a separate {{filegroup}} on another disk.

def: A log recording changes before they are applied for crash recovery.
ex: The {{write-ahead log}} lets the database recover after a sudden crash.

def: A saved point where in-memory changes are written to disk.
ex: A {{checkpoint}} flushes buffered data, speeding future recovery.

def: Memory holding recently used data pages to reduce disk reads.
ex: A larger {{buffer pool}} keeps more pages in memory for fast access.

def: Processing large volumes of data together in scheduled groups.
ex: Nightly {{batch processing}} updates every account balance at once.

def: A series of steps that move and transform data automatically.
ex: The {{data pipeline}} loads raw logs into the analytics database.

def: Extracting, transforming, and loading data into a target system.
ex: An {{ETL}} job cleans the data before loading the warehouse.

def: Storing table data by column instead of by row for analytics.
ex: {{Columnar storage}} makes summing a single column extremely fast.

def: A guarantee that all users see the same valid database state.
ex: Strong {{consistency}} ensures every replica returns identical results.

def: A system's ability to handle growth in data or load.
ex: Sharding improved the database's {{scalability}} across many servers.

def: A static package with code, libraries and settings used to run a container.
ex: They pushed the new {{container image}} to the registry after testing.

def: A set of machines managed together to run containerized workloads at scale.
ex: The {{Kubernetes cluster}} automatically restarts any failed pods.

def: The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes holding one or more containers.
ex: Each {{pod}} shares the same network address inside the cluster.

def: A group of machines with the same configuration within a cluster.
ex: We added a GPU {{node pool}} for the machine learning jobs.

def: Automatically adjusting compute resources up or down based on current demand.
ex: {{Auto-scaling}} added more servers when traffic spiked at noon.

def: A managed service spreading incoming traffic across many backend targets automatically.
ex: The {{elastic load balancer}} routed requests to the healthiest instances.

def: An isolated data center location within a cloud region for fault tolerance.
ex: They spread replicas across every {{availability zone}} for resilience.

def: A geographic area containing multiple cloud data centers and services.
ex: They deployed the app to the European {{region}} for lower latency.

def: Managing infrastructure through machine-readable definition files instead of manual setup.
ex: With {{infrastructure as code}} they rebuild environments in one command.

def: Maintaining consistent settings across servers and systems over time.
ex: {{Configuration management}} keeps every server identical across the fleet.

def: A distinct step in an automated build, test or release workflow.
ex: The tests run in a separate {{pipeline stage}} before deployment.

def: A storage service for versioned build outputs like images and packages.
ex: Compiled binaries are stored in the {{artifact registry}} for reuse.

def: Releasing by switching traffic between two identical environments to avoid downtime.
ex: A {{blue-green deployment}} let them roll back instantly if needed.

def: Gradually rolling out a change to a small subset of users first.
ex: The {{canary release}} exposed the bug before all users saw it.

def: A layer that manages communication, security and routing between microservices.
ex: The {{service mesh}} encrypts all traffic between internal services.

def: Incoming network traffic entering a cluster or system from outside.
ex: An {{ingress}} controller routes external requests to the right service.

def: Outgoing network traffic leaving a cluster or system to elsewhere.
ex: Cloud providers often charge extra for data {{egress}} between regions.

def: Securely storing and controlling access to passwords, keys and tokens.
ex: Proper {{secret management}} keeps API keys out of the source code.

def: A set of tools for monitoring metrics, logs and traces together.
ex: Their {{observability stack}} shows exactly where requests slow down.

def: Collecting log data from many sources into one searchable place.
ex: {{Log aggregation}} let the team search every server from one dashboard.

def: Following a single request as it travels across multiple services.
ex: {{Distributed tracing}} revealed the slow call to the payment service.

def: A small independent service that performs one specific business function.
ex: Each {{microservice}} can be deployed and scaled on its own.

def: A single large application where all functions are tightly combined together.
ex: They split the old {{monolith}} into several smaller services.

def: Frequently merging code changes and running automated builds and tests.
ex: {{Continuous integration}} catches broken code within minutes of a commit.

def: Keeping software always ready to release through automated pipelines.
ex: {{Continuous delivery}} means any commit could ship to production today.

def: Replacing instances gradually so the service stays available during changes.
ex: A {{rolling update}} upgraded the pods without any downtime.

def: Adding more machines to share the load rather than enlarging one.
ex: {{Horizontal scaling}} handled the traffic by adding ten more servers.

def: Increasing the power of a single machine by adding more resources.
ex: {{Vertical scaling}} gave the database more memory and CPU cores.

def: A repository that stores and distributes container images to systems.
ex: They pulled the base image from a private {{image registry}}.

def: A text file with instructions for building a container image.
ex: The {{Dockerfile}} lists every step needed to build the image.

def: A declarative file describing the desired state of resources to deploy.
ex: The {{manifest}} tells Kubernetes how many replicas to run.

def: Describing a service that keeps no client data between requests.
ex: A {{stateless}} service is easy to scale and replace.

def: Describing a service that retains data between requests or sessions.
ex: A {{stateful}} database needs careful handling during upgrades.

def: Cluster storage that keeps data even when a pod restarts.
ex: The database writes to a {{persistent volume}} so nothing is lost.

def: A physical server used directly without any virtualization layer.
ex: For maximum performance they ran the database on {{bare metal}}.

def: Serving multiple customers from one shared software instance securely.
ex: {{Multi-tenancy}} lets many clients share the same platform safely.

def: The ability to grow or shrink resources quickly as demand changes.
ex: Cloud {{elasticity}} handled the holiday shopping surge smoothly.

def: Built specifically to run and scale in cloud environments.
ex: The {{cloud-native}} app scales automatically without manual work.

def: An automated message sent to a URL when an event happens.
ex: A {{webhook}} triggers the pipeline whenever new code is pushed.

def: A test that confirms whether a service is running correctly.
ex: The load balancer removes any instance that fails a {{health check}}.

def: Able to detect and fix failures automatically without human help.
ex: The {{self-healing}} cluster replaced the crashed pod on its own.

def: Producing the same result no matter how many times it runs.
ex: An {{idempotent}} script can rerun safely without side effects.

def: Restricting how many requests a client may send in a period.
ex: {{Rate limiting}} stops one user from overwhelming the API.

def: A single entry point that routes and manages requests to services.
ex: The {{API gateway}} handles authentication before forwarding requests.

def: A server that forwards client requests to one or more backends.
ex: A {{reverse proxy}} hides the internal servers from the internet.

def: A helper container running alongside the main application container.
ex: A {{sidecar}} handles logging so the main app stays simple.

def: A container that runs setup tasks before the main container starts.
ex: The {{init container}} prepares the database before the app launches.

def: Gradual divergence of real infrastructure from its defined configuration.
ex: Configuration {{drift}} caused one server to behave differently.

def: An automated script that installs and configures a new machine.
ex: A {{provisioning script}} sets up each server the same way.

def: Replacing servers entirely on change rather than modifying them in place.
ex: With {{immutable infrastructure}} they rebuild servers instead of patching.

def: Moving applications and data from local systems into the cloud.
ex: The {{cloud migration}} moved all workloads off the old servers.

def: The delay when an idle function starts up on first request.
ex: A {{cold start}} added a second of latency to the first call.

def: Pre-started instances kept ready to serve traffic instantly.
ex: A {{warm pool}} keeps spare servers ready for sudden spikes.

def: A cheap temporary server that the provider may reclaim anytime.
ex: They ran batch jobs on a {{spot instance}} to cut costs.

def: A customer or group sharing a system while staying isolated.
ex: Each {{tenant}} sees only its own data on the platform.

def: A documented set of steps for handling a routine operational task.
ex: The on-call engineer followed the {{runbook}} to restart the service.

def: The coordinated process of handling and resolving a system outage.
ex: Fast {{incident response}} restored the site within ten minutes.

def: A contract defining the expected availability and performance of a service.
ex: Their {{service level agreement}} promises 99.9 percent uptime monthly.

def: Software that automates deploying, scaling and managing many containers.
ex: The {{container orchestrator}} restarts failed containers automatically.

def: An automated path that moves code from commit to production.
ex: Every change flows through the {{deployment pipeline}} before going live.

def: A switch that turns a feature on or off without redeploying.
ex: A {{feature flag}} let them release the design to selected users.

def: Deliberately breaking systems to test how well they recover.
ex: {{Chaos engineering}} exposed a weakness by killing random servers.

def: A visual display of key performance numbers for a system.
ex: The {{metrics dashboard}} showed CPU usage climbing sharply.

def: Automatically notifying engineers when a system crosses a threshold.
ex: {{Alerting}} paged the team the moment error rates rose.

def: A company that supplies cloud computing services and infrastructure.
ex: They compared each {{provider}} on price and available regions.

def: Labeling cloud resources with metadata for organization and billing.
ex: Consistent {{tagging}} made it easy to track costs per team.

def: A limit on how much of a resource an account may use.
ex: They hit the {{quota}} on virtual machines and requested more.

def: A backup system kept partly running to take over quickly.
ex: A {{warm standby}} in another region took over during the outage.

def: A proposal to merge code changes reviewed before being accepted.
ex: She opened a {{pull request}} so teammates could review her code.

def: A system tracking changes to code so history can be reviewed.
ex: {{Version control}} let them revert to yesterday's working code.

def: Web layout approach that adjusts automatically to different screen sizes and devices.
ex: The site uses {{responsive design}} to look good on phones and desktops.

def: Able to run or work on several operating systems or device types.
ex: They built a {{cross-platform}} app that runs on iOS and Android.

def: Application built specifically for one operating system using its own tools.
ex: A {{native app}} usually feels faster than a web-based one.

def: Website that behaves like an installable app with offline and push features.
ex: A {{progressive web app}} can work even without an internet connection.

def: User permission required before a website stores tracking cookies.
ex: European sites must show a {{cookie consent}} banner to visitors.

def: Process of removing outdated stored data so fresh content is served.
ex: Bad {{cache invalidation}} caused users to see old prices.

def: Distributed servers that deliver web content quickly from nearby locations.
ex: A {{content delivery network}} speeds up loading for global visitors.

def: Entry that maps a domain name to an address or service.
ex: Update the {{DNS record}} to point the domain to the new server.

def: Digital file that enables encrypted, trusted connections between browser and site.
ex: Install an {{SSL certificate}} to enable a secure padlock.

def: Message a client sends to a server asking for a resource.
ex: Each page load sends several {{HTTP request}} messages to the server.

def: Numeric server response indicating the result of a web request.
ex: A 404 {{status code}} means the page was not found.

def: The actual data carried inside a request or message.
ex: The API returned a JSON {{payload}} with user details.

def: Message sent to a device even when the app is closed.
ex: The app sends a {{push notification}} when a new message arrives.

def: Identifying the real-world position of a device or user.
ex: The map uses {{geolocation}} to show nearby restaurants.

def: Short-range wireless technology for connecting devices over small distances.
ex: Pair your headphones with the phone over {{Bluetooth}}.

def: Wireless technology enabling data exchange between devices held very close.
ex: Contactless payment relies on {{near-field communication}} between the card and reader.

def: Network of everyday physical objects connected to the internet.
ex: The {{Internet of Things}} lets your fridge order milk automatically.

def: Small computer built into a device to control a specific function.
ex: A washing machine runs on a simple {{embedded system}}.

def: Device that detects physical input like light, motion, or temperature.
ex: A motion {{sensor}} turns the lights on automatically.

def: Wireless delivery of firmware updates directly to a device.
ex: The car received a {{firmware over the air}} update overnight.

def: Electronic device that connects to networks and works interactively.
ex: A {{smart device}} like a thermostat can be controlled by phone.

def: Computing point near the data source rather than a central server.
ex: Processing at an {{edge node}} reduces delay for local users.

def: Web app that loads one page and updates content dynamically.
ex: A {{single-page application}} feels smooth because it avoids full reloads.

def: Visible area of a web page on a device screen.
ex: Set the {{viewport}} meta tag so mobile pages scale correctly.

def: Screen width where a responsive layout changes its arrangement.
ex: Add a {{breakpoint}} at 768 pixels for tablet layouts.

def: Small icon shown in a browser tab representing a website.
ex: Upload a {{favicon}} so your site looks polished in tabs.

def: Three-line icon that opens a hidden navigation menu on mobile.
ex: Tap the {{hamburger menu}} to see all the app sections.

def: Quick touch on a screen to select or activate something.
ex: A single {{tap gesture}} opens the selected photo.

def: Sliding finger movement across a touchscreen to navigate or dismiss.
ex: You can {{swipe}} left to delete the message from the list.

def: Sensor that measures a device's movement and orientation.
ex: The {{accelerometer}} rotates the screen when you tilt the phone.

def: Sensor that detects rotation and helps track device orientation.
ex: Racing games use the {{gyroscope}} to steer by tilting.

def: Technology that overlays digital images onto the real world.
ex: An {{augmented reality}} app shows furniture in your actual room.

def: Computer-generated environment users experience through a headset.
ex: A {{virtual reality}} headset immerses you in a digital world.

def: Smart electronic device worn on the body, like a watch.
ex: A {{wearable}} tracks your heart rate throughout the day.

def: Small transmitter that sends signals to nearby mobile devices.
ex: A shop {{beacon}} sends discount offers to passing phones.

def: Square barcode scanned by a camera to open links or data.
ex: Scan the {{QR code}} on the menu to view the drinks.

def: Identity verification using physical traits like fingerprints or faces.
ex: The phone unlocks with {{biometric authentication}} instead of a code.

def: Sensor that reads a fingerprint to verify a user's identity.
ex: Place your thumb on the {{fingerprint scanner}} to log in.

def: Technology that identifies a person by analysing their face.
ex: The app uses {{facial recognition}} to unlock the screen instantly.

def: Interface colour scheme using dark backgrounds to reduce eye strain.
ex: Enable {{dark mode}} to make reading at night easier.

def: App state that works without an active internet connection.
ex: Download the maps so {{offline mode}} still shows directions.

def: Link that opens a specific screen inside a mobile app.
ex: The email uses a {{deep link}} to open the offer page directly.

def: Online platform where users download and install mobile applications.
ex: Publish the game on the {{app store}} to reach more users.

def: Buying extra content or features from inside an application.
ex: The free game offers an {{in-app purchase}} for extra coins.

def: Guided introduction that helps new users start using an app.
ex: A short {{onboarding}} tutorial explains the main features.

def: Opening screen shown briefly while an app finishes loading.
ex: The {{splash screen}} displays the logo before the menu appears.

def: Software that imitates one device or system on another.
ex: Test the app on an Android {{emulator}} before release.

def: Set of rules letting different software programs communicate with each other.
ex: The weather {{application programming interface}} returns data for any city.

def: Specific address where an API can be accessed by a client.
ex: Send the request to the login {{endpoint}} of the API.

def: Lightweight text format for storing and exchanging structured data.
ex: The server returns user data in {{JSON}} format.

def: Coding system that uses tags to structure text and content.
ex: HTML is a {{markup language}} used to build web pages.

def: File that defines the appearance and layout of web pages.
ex: Edit the {{style sheet}} to change the site's colours.

def: Software toolkit that simplifies building websites and web applications.
ex: They chose a modern {{web framework}} to speed up development.

def: Standalone web page designed to capture visitors for a campaign.
ex: The ad sends users to a focused {{landing page}}.

def: Program that automatically browses the web to index pages.
ex: A search engine {{web crawler}} visits your site regularly.

def: Improving a website so it ranks higher in search results.
ex: Good {{search engine optimization}} brings more visitors from Google.

def: Overall feeling and ease a person has when using a product.
ex: A confusing menu ruins the {{user experience}}.

def: Visual part of a program that people interact with directly.
ex: The app has a clean {{user interface}} that is easy to learn.

def: Simple visual blueprint showing the layout of a page.
ex: Designers drew a {{wireframe}} before building the real screen.

def: Detailed visual model of a design before it is built.
ex: The client approved the {{mockup}} of the home page.

def: Early working model used to test an idea or design.
ex: They built a clickable {{prototype}} to gather feedback.

def: Creating a virtual boundary that triggers actions on entry or exit.
ex: Retail {{geofencing}} sends a coupon when you enter the mall.

def: Automatic collection and transmission of data from remote devices.
ex: The app sends usage {{telemetry}} to improve performance.

def: Component that turns a control signal into physical movement.
ex: The smart lock uses an {{actuator}} to move the bolt.

def: Tiny computer chip that controls a specific electronic function.
ex: An Arduino board is powered by a {{microcontroller}}.

def: Network where devices connect directly and relay data for each other.
ex: Smart bulbs form a {{mesh network}} to extend coverage.

def: Device setting that limits activity to save battery power.
ex: Switch on {{low-power mode}} when the battery is nearly empty.

def: Fifth-generation mobile network offering very fast wireless speeds.
ex: With {{5G}} you can download a movie in seconds.

def: Wireless network of towers that connects mobile phones.
ex: Calls travel over the {{cellular network}} between towers.

def: Small chip that identifies a phone on a cellular network.
ex: Insert a new {{SIM card}} to use a local number abroad.

def: Sharing a phone's internet connection with other devices.
ex: Use {{tethering}} to give your laptop internet from your phone.

def: Virtual copy of a real object used for simulation and monitoring.
ex: Engineers test the engine using its {{digital twin}}.

def: Direct communication between devices without human involvement.
ex: A {{machine-to-machine}} link lets sensors report data automatically.

def: A site's ability to work correctly across different web browsers.
ex: Test for {{cross-browser compatibility}} in Chrome, Safari, and Firefox.

def: Loading content only when it is actually needed on screen.
ex: Using {{lazy loading}} for images makes the page open faster.

def: Attack that injects malicious code into a trusted web page.
ex: Validate input to prevent {{cross-site scripting}} attacks.
